questions.q: Explain the MVC architecture and how a request flows through Model-View-Controller.

answer.oneLiner: MVC splits your app into three parts: the Model holds data and business rules, the View renders the UI, and the Controller handles the request and connects the two. A request enters at the Controller, the Controller fetches data from the Model, then passes that data to the View to produce the response.
answer.detail: When a browser sends a request, ASP.NET Core routing inspects the URL and maps it to a specific controller action method. The controller is instantiated and the action runs. Inside the action, the controller calls services or repositories (the Model layer) to get or change data. Once data is ready, the controller picks a View and passes the data to it as a ViewModel or model object. The Razor engine processes the View template and produces HTML. That HTML is sent back to the browser. The key insight is that each layer has one job: routing maps URLs, controllers coordinate, models own logic and data, views own presentation. This separation means you can test the business logic in the model layer without spinning up a web server.
keyPoints: Routing maps the incoming URL to a specific controller action - this is the entry point.
Controllers are coordinators only - they must not contain business logic.
Business logic belongs in the service or domain model layer, not in the controller.
The View only displays data - it should never call a database directly.
Each layer is independently testable because of this clear separation.

code.note: The controller action shrinks to two lines when logic moves to the service - this is the correct MVC pattern.

answer.analogy: The controller is a waiter: takes the order, fetches from the kitchen, never cooks.
redFlags: Saying the View can query the database directly.
Putting business rules like pricing or shipping logic inside a controller action.
Not knowing that routing happens before the controller is called.
Confusing the Model (business/data layer) with a database table.

followUpsQA.q: What is a fat controller and why is it bad?
followUpsQA.a: A fat controller puts business logic directly in the action method. It breaks separation of concerns and makes that logic impossible to test without an HTTP context.

followUpsQA.q: What maps a URL to a controller action?
followUpsQA.a: The routing middleware maps the URL pattern to a controller name and action name before the controller is even instantiated.

followUpsQA.q: How do you pass data from a controller to a view?
followUpsQA.a: You pass a strongly-typed ViewModel as the argument to the View() call, giving the Razor template compile-time type safety.

followUpsQA.q: Can MVC handle API responses as well as HTML views?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - returning Ok(), NotFound(), or Json() from an action produces an API response instead of a Razor view, using the same controller and routing infrastructure.

followUpsQA.q: How does MVC improve testability?
followUpsQA.a: Because the Model layer has no dependency on HTTP, you can unit test business logic by calling service methods directly with no web server needed.

dialogue.text: Walk me through MVC architecture and how a request flows through it.

A browser request hits the routing middleware first, which maps the URL to a specific controller action. The controller runs that action, calls the service layer to get data, then passes the data to a View. The Razor engine renders the HTML and sends it back. The split means each layer has exactly one job.
What happens in the Model layer specifically?

The Model is your business and data logic - services, domain objects, repositories. A shipping service deciding when to mark an order as dispatched is Model logic. The controller just calls ShipOrder() and passes the result to the view. Nothing about HTTP lives in the service.
What is the classic mistake developers make with MVC?

The fat controller - putting business rules directly in the action method. I have seen controllers with 200 lines of pricing and inventory logic. It makes that code untestable and unreusable. The fix is to move everything into a service class and call it from the action.
How does MVC compare to Razor Pages for a CRUD admin panel?

For a simple CRUD admin panel, Razor Pages can be cleaner - each page gets its own PageModel with OnGet and OnPost handlers instead of a shared controller managing many views. MVC shines when you need complex routing, API endpoints alongside views, or you have a large team familiar with the pattern.
How do you test controller logic without running a web server?

I test the service layer directly - that is where the logic lives, so no HTTP context is needed. For the controller itself, I mock the service with Moq, call the action method directly, and assert on the IActionResult type and value. ControllerContext can be set manually if the action reads HttpContext.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Compare ViewBag, ViewData, TempData, and a ViewModel for passing data to a view.

answer.oneLiner: A ViewModel is a strongly-typed C# class and is the best practice for passing data to a view. ViewData and ViewBag are loosely-typed dictionaries that only live for the current request. TempData is also a dictionary but survives one redirect.
answer.detail: ViewData is a dictionary of type Dictionary<string, object> accessible in the controller and in the view. You write ViewData["Title"] = "Checkout" in the controller and read it in the Razor template. ViewBag is a dynamic wrapper over the same ViewData dictionary - writing ViewBag.Title = "Checkout" stores the value in exactly the same place as ViewData["Title"]. Both are gone after the current request is done. TempData is different - it is backed by a session or cookie and survives until it is read in the next request, making it useful for post-redirect-get flash messages like 'Your order was saved'. A ViewModel is a plain C# class with typed properties. The controller creates an instance, populates it, and passes it to View(model). The Razor template declares @model OrderSummaryViewModel and gets full IntelliSense and compile-time checking. This is always the preferred approach for real application screens.
keyPoints: ViewModel is a typed C# class - you get compile-time checking, IntelliSense, and refactor safety.
ViewBag and ViewData share the same underlying dictionary - they are the same data store.
ViewBag and ViewData only exist for the current request - they vanish after the response is sent.
TempData persists across exactly one redirect - perfect for post-redirect-get success messages.
ViewBag and ViewData failures are runtime errors; ViewModel failures are caught at compile time.

code.note: A typo in a ViewBag property name produces a silent null; the same typo in a ViewModel property is a build error.

answer.analogy: ViewBag is a sticky note on the door; ViewModel is a typed form handed directly to the clerk.
redFlags: Saying ViewBag and ViewData are different storage mechanisms - they share the same dictionary.
Not knowing that TempData requires session or cookie to be configured.
Confusing TempData lifetime with ViewData lifetime - TempData survives the redirect, ViewData does not.
Recommending ViewBag as a best practice for passing data to views.

followUpsQA.q: What is the underlying type of ViewData?
followUpsQA.a: ViewData is of type ViewDataDictionary which is a Dictionary<string, object>. ViewBag is a DynamicViewData wrapper over the exact same dictionary instance.

followUpsQA.q: When should you use TempData?
followUpsQA.a: Use TempData for post-redirect-get messages - for example after a customer places an order, you redirect to an order confirmation page and TempData carries the 'Order placed successfully' message across the redirect.

followUpsQA.q: What backing store does TempData use?
followUpsQA.a: By default, ASP.NET Core uses a cookie-based TempData provider. You can switch to session-based storage by calling AddSessionStateTempDataProvider() in your DI setup.

followUpsQA.q: Can a single view have both a ViewModel and TempData?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - you would use the ViewModel for the main page data and read TempData for a one-time flash message like a success or error notification.

followUpsQA.q: Why does ViewBag fail silently with typos but ViewModel does not?
followUpsQA.a: ViewBag uses C# dynamic which defers property resolution to runtime. A ViewModel property access is resolved by the C# compiler at build time, so a typo is a compile error.

dialogue.text: Compare ViewBag, ViewData, TempData, and a ViewModel for passing data to a view.

A ViewModel is the right tool for most cases - it is a typed C# class, so you get IntelliSense and compile-time safety. ViewData and ViewBag are both dictionary-based and only live for the current request. TempData is also a dictionary but it survives one redirect, which makes it useful for flash messages in a post-redirect-get flow.
You said ViewBag and ViewData are both dictionaries - are they the same thing?

Yes, exactly the same underlying store. ViewBag is a dynamic wrapper over ViewData. Writing ViewBag.Title stores the value in ViewData under the key 'Title'. They are two syntaxes for one dictionary. ViewBag just looks cleaner because you skip the string key.
Give me a real scenario where TempData is the correct choice.

Post-redirect-get pattern in an e-commerce checkout. After a customer places an order, you POST to /Order/Place, save to the database, then redirect to /Order/Confirmation. TempData carries 'Your order #1234 is confirmed' across that redirect and is cleared automatically after the confirmation page reads it.
What is the risk of relying on ViewBag in a large team?

A typo in a ViewBag property name like ViewBag.OrdrTotal silently returns null at runtime instead of failing at build time. On a large team with many views, these bugs are hard to trace. A ViewModel makes a typo a compile error caught in the IDE before deployment.
Where does TempData actually store its data?

By default, ASP.NET Core uses a cookie-based TempData provider - the value is Base64-encoded into a response cookie and read back on the next request. You can swap to session storage by registering AddSessionStateTempDataProvider() in Program.cs if cookie size is a concern.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is a partial view and how do you pass data from it back to a controller?

answer.oneLiner: A partial view is a reusable piece of Razor markup stored in its own .cshtml file and embedded inside other views. To send its input data back to a controller, you place the partial's form fields inside an HTML form that POSTs to a controller action, and model binding maps the posted values to the action's parameter.
answer.detail: A partial view is rendered using the <partial> tag helper or Html.PartialAsync() and it receives its own model object. It is purely a rendering concern - it does not have a lifecycle of its own for posting data. When you need user input from a partial view (such as a comment form embedded in a product detail page), you wrap the partial inside a <form> tag in the parent view. The form's action attribute points to a controller action. When the user submits, the browser collects all input field values from the entire form - including those rendered by the partial - and sends them as a POST body. ASP.NET Core model binding reads that POST body and maps field names to properties on the action's parameter type. The partial itself has no special role in this round-trip; it is just a way to organise the Razor markup into smaller files.
keyPoints: A partial view is a reusable .cshtml fragment - it has no special posting mechanism of its own.
Use <partial name='_PartialName' model='viewModel' /> or Html.PartialAsync to render it.
Data goes back to the controller via a standard HTML form POST, not via the partial.
Model binding maps the POSTed field names to the action parameter's properties by matching names.
The partial and the parent view share the same form submission - there is no separate HTTP call.

code.note: The partial renders the input fields; the surrounding form is what actually sends data to the controller action.

answer.analogy: The partial is the form template on paper; the envelope (the HTML form) is what gets mailed to the controller.
redFlags: Saying a partial view can POST data to a controller by itself without a form.
Confusing a partial view with a View Component - a View Component has its own logic and data fetching.
Not knowing that model binding matches POST field names to parameter property names by convention.
Thinking you need JavaScript to send partial view data back - a standard form POST is sufficient.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between a partial view and a View Component?
followUpsQA.a: A partial view is pure Razor markup with an optional typed model passed in. A View Component has its own InvokeAsync method with injected dependencies, so it can fetch its own data independently of the parent controller.

followUpsQA.q: Can you nest partial views?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - a partial view can render another partial view. There is no limit, but deep nesting makes debugging harder and should be kept to a reasonable depth.

followUpsQA.q: How does model binding know which field belongs to which property?
followUpsQA.a: The input name attributes generated by tag helpers like asp-for='Text' match the property path on the ViewModel. Model binding reads the POST body key-value pairs and maps by name, ignoring case.

followUpsQA.q: Can a partial view be rendered via AJAX?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - a controller action can return PartialView() and you fetch it with fetch() or jQuery.load(), then insert the HTML into the page. This is the standard pattern for dynamic content without a full page reload.

followUpsQA.q: What tag helper do you use to render a partial view?
followUpsQA.a: The <partial name='_PartialName' model='someModel' /> tag helper. You can also use await Html.PartialAsync() in code blocks for more control.

dialogue.text: What is a partial view and how do you get data from it back to the controller?

A partial view is a reusable Razor .cshtml fragment rendered inside a parent view using the partial tag helper or Html.PartialAsync. It has no posting mechanism of its own. To get data back to the controller, you embed the partial inside a form that POSTs to an action, and model binding maps the field names to the action parameter.
So the partial is not doing the posting at all?

Exactly - the partial just renders input fields. It is the HTML form wrapping the partial that sends the HTTP POST. Model binding then reads the POST body and matches field names to ViewModel properties by name convention. The partial is just a way to split the Razor markup into manageable files.
What is the difference between a partial view and a View Component?

A partial view is passive - you pass it a model from the parent. A View Component has its own InvokeAsync method with injected services, so it fetches its own data. On a product page, a 'Recently Viewed Items' widget would be a View Component because it needs its own database query, not a model from the product controller.
Can you load a partial view via AJAX to update part of the page?

Yes - a controller action can return PartialView('_ReviewList', reviews). The client calls it with fetch(), gets back HTML, and inserts it into the DOM. This avoids a full page reload and is the standard pattern for dynamic sections in MVC apps.
How does model binding match the form fields to the right ViewModel properties?

Tag helpers like asp-for='Review.Text' generate an input with name='Review.Text'. Model binding reads the POST body, matches that name to the Text property on the Review property of the action parameter, and sets the value. It is case-insensitive and handles nested objects with dot notation.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between a Razor View (MVC) and a Razor Page?

answer.oneLiner: A Razor View belongs to a controller - the controller handles the request and decides which view to render. A Razor Page is self-contained: the .cshtml file and its .cshtml.cs PageModel are paired together, with the PageModel handling requests directly using OnGet and OnPost methods.
answer.detail: In MVC, one controller class can serve many views. The controller action decides what data to fetch and which view to render. The view itself has no request-handling logic. In Razor Pages, the model is inverted: the page is the unit of organisation. Every .cshtml Razor Page has a matching .cshtml.cs file containing a PageModel class. The PageModel has an OnGet method for GET requests and OnPost for POST requests. There is no separate controller. Routing is file-system based - /Pages/Orders/Index.cshtml is served at /Orders. This makes Razor Pages natural for page-centric, CRUD-style UIs where each URL corresponds to one feature. MVC remains the better fit when you have complex routing, many API endpoints alongside views, or a large existing MVC codebase. Both use the same Razor syntax and can coexist in one ASP.NET Core application.
keyPoints: MVC: one controller action returns one of many possible views - controller is the entry point.
Razor Pages: each page has its own PageModel class as the entry point - no separate controller.
Razor Page routing is file-system based - the folder path maps directly to the URL.
PageModel uses OnGet, OnPost, OnPut handler methods instead of action methods.
Both use the same Razor syntax and both run on the same ASP.NET Core pipeline.

code.note: [BindProperty] on a PageModel property auto-binds POST data without needing a separate parameter.

answer.analogy: MVC is a restaurant with one head chef (controller) sending dishes to many tables (views). Razor Pages gives every table its own mini-chef.
redFlags: Saying Razor Pages and MVC Views use different templating syntax - both use Razor.
Not knowing that [BindProperty] eliminates the need for a separate POST parameter in Razor Pages.
Saying Razor Pages cannot coexist with MVC in the same application.
Confusing Razor Pages PageModel with a ViewModel - PageModel is the handler, ViewModel is just data.

followUpsQA.q: How does routing work in Razor Pages vs MVC?
followUpsQA.a: Razor Pages uses file-system routing by default - /Pages/Products/Index.cshtml serves the /Products URL. MVC uses attribute or conventional routing defined in Program.cs and can map any URL pattern to any controller action.

followUpsQA.q: What is [BindProperty] in Razor Pages?
followUpsQA.a: [BindProperty] on a PageModel property tells the framework to automatically bind that property from the POST body. This means you do not need a separate method parameter for the posted data - the property is populated before OnPost runs.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use dependency injection in a Razor Pages PageModel?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - exactly like a controller. You inject services through the PageModel constructor. The DI container wires everything up the same way.

followUpsQA.q: When would you choose MVC over Razor Pages?
followUpsQA.a: Choose MVC when you need complex or attribute-based routing, when you are building a large API surface alongside views, or when you already have an established MVC codebase. Razor Pages is simpler for page-centric CRUD screens.

followUpsQA.q: Can Razor Pages and MVC Controllers coexist in one ASP.NET Core app?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - you add both services.AddControllersWithViews() and services.AddRazorPages() in Program.cs and both work on the same request pipeline.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between a Razor View in MVC and a Razor Page?

In MVC, the controller is the entry point - it handles the request and returns a view. The view is passive. In Razor Pages, the page itself is the unit of organisation. Each .cshtml page has a paired .cshtml.cs PageModel class with OnGet and OnPost methods. There is no separate controller class.
How does routing differ between the two?

Razor Pages uses file-system routing by default. If I create /Pages/Orders/Edit.cshtml, it is automatically served at /Orders/Edit. MVC routing is defined separately in Program.cs and you can map any URL pattern to any controller and action independently of the file location.
What is [BindProperty] and why does it matter in Razor Pages?

[BindProperty] on a PageModel property tells the framework to populate it from the POST body before OnPost runs. So instead of writing public IActionResult OnPost(OrderViewModel vm), you declare [BindProperty] public OrderViewModel Order { get; set; } and it is populated automatically. This removes duplication between OnGet and OnPost.
When would you pick MVC over Razor Pages for a new project?

I would pick MVC when I need to serve both API endpoints and HTML views from the same controller, or when the routing requirements are complex enough to need attribute routing with custom constraints. For a pure CRUD admin dashboard, I would use Razor Pages because it is less ceremonial.
Can you mix both in one application?

Yes - you register both in Program.cs: builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews() for MVC and builder.Services.AddRazorPages() for Razor Pages. Both run on the same pipeline. A common pattern is Razor Pages for public-facing screens and MVC controllers for the REST API endpoints.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are the common IActionResult return types in ASP.NET Core MVC?

answer.oneLiner: IActionResult is the return type contract that lets one action method return different outcomes - a rendered view, a redirect, a JSON payload, a file download, or an HTTP status code. The framework calls ExecuteResultAsync on whatever concrete result you return.
answer.detail: IActionResult is an interface with one method: ExecuteResultAsync. Every concrete result type implements it. View() returns a ViewResult that finds and renders the Razor template. RedirectToAction() and Redirect() return redirect results that send a 302 response to another URL. Ok(), Created(), NotFound(), and BadRequest() return ObjectResult subclasses with specific HTTP status codes, typically used in API controllers. Json() returns a JsonResult that serialises the object to JSON. File() returns a FileResult for binary downloads. The benefit of returning IActionResult is that a single action can branch: return the view on validation failure, or redirect on success. In typed controllers (those returning ActionResult<T>), the framework can also generate OpenAPI documentation knowing the response shape. Using the concrete type (returning ViewResult directly) is valid but limits flexibility.
keyPoints: View() - returns ViewResult, which finds and renders the Razor .cshtml template.
RedirectToAction() / Redirect() - returns a 302 redirect response to another action or URL.
Ok() / NotFound() / BadRequest() - returns status-code results used in API actions.
Json() - serialises an object to JSON with status 200.
IActionResult as a return type allows an action to return different result types depending on the outcome.

code.note: Returning IActionResult lets one action method produce a view on failure and a redirect on success without any casting.

answer.analogy: IActionResult is the universal output socket; each return type is a different plug that fits it.
redFlags: Not knowing that Ok(), NotFound(), and BadRequest() exist - calling them only by status code number.
Returning a concrete result type like ViewResult and then being unable to explain why that limits the action.
Confusing Json() with Ok(object) - both return JSON but Ok() is the correct API idiom that signals 200.
Saying IActionResult is only for API controllers - it is used in both MVC and API actions.

followUpsQA.q: What is ActionResult<T> and when do you use it?
followUpsQA.a: ActionResult<T> is a generic wrapper that lets you return either T directly or any IActionResult. It is used in API controllers so Swagger/OpenAPI can infer the response body type from T while still allowing you to return NotFound() or BadRequest() for error cases.

followUpsQA.q: What HTTP status code does Ok() return?
followUpsQA.a: Ok() returns HTTP 200. Created() returns 201. NoContent() returns 204. NotFound() returns 404. BadRequest() returns 400.

followUpsQA.q: How do you return a file download from an action?
followUpsQA.a: Use File(bytes, contentType, fileName) which returns a FileResult. The framework sets the Content-Disposition header to attachment, which tells the browser to download rather than display the file.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between Redirect() and RedirectToAction()?
followUpsQA.a: Redirect() sends the user to a hardcoded URL string. RedirectToAction() builds the URL by resolving the controller and action names through routing, so it stays correct if you change route configuration.

followUpsQA.q: When should you use NoContent() instead of Ok()?
followUpsQA.a: Use NoContent() (204) when the action succeeds but there is no body to return - such as a DELETE endpoint that removes a record. Ok() implies a response body; NoContent() explicitly signals there is none.

dialogue.text: Walk me through the common IActionResult return types in ASP.NET Core.

View() renders a Razor template and returns 200 HTML. RedirectToAction and Redirect return 302 redirects. Ok, NotFound, BadRequest, and Created are status-code results used in API actions. Json returns a serialised JSON body. File returns binary data with a Content-Disposition header for downloads. The whole point of IActionResult as the return type is that one action can branch across any of these.
Why return IActionResult instead of a concrete type like ViewResult?

Because a real action usually has multiple outcomes. On a save form: if ModelState is invalid, return View(vm) to show errors. If the save succeeds, return RedirectToAction to the list page. If I lock the return type to ViewResult, I cannot return a redirect without a compile error.
What is ActionResult<T> and how does it differ from IActionResult?

ActionResult<T> is a generic wrapper used in API controllers. It lets you return T directly - the serialiser wraps it in a 200 - or any IActionResult like NotFound() for error cases. The bonus is that Swagger reads T and generates the correct response schema in the OpenAPI spec automatically.
When do you use NoContent versus Ok in an API?

NoContent (204) is for operations that succeed but produce nothing - a DELETE endpoint is the classic example. Ok (200) is for operations that return data. Returning Ok() with no body is technically valid but sending 204 is more semantically correct and some HTTP clients check for it.
How do you return a PDF invoice as a file download?

Use File(pdfBytes, "application/pdf", "invoice-12345.pdf"). The framework sets Content-Type to application/pdf and Content-Disposition to attachment with the filename. The browser sees the attachment header and prompts the download dialog instead of trying to render the PDF inline.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Scenario: a date posted from a form makes the entire bound model null. What's going on?

answer.oneLiner: When a date string from the form cannot be parsed by the model binder, the binding for that field fails. If the DateTime property is non-nullable and the binder cannot set it, the entire model can come back as null or the ModelState will contain an error - depending on the binding mode and whether the property is required.
answer.detail: ASP.NET Core model binding tries to convert each posted string value to the target property type. For DateTime, the binder uses the current request culture to parse the string. A UK browser may post '21/06/2026' (dd/MM/yyyy) but the server culture may expect 'MM/dd/yyyy', causing a parse failure. When a required or non-nullable DateTime property fails to bind, the binder adds an error to ModelState and leaves the model in a partially constructed state. In some configurations - particularly with complex nested models - a single property binding failure can cause the entire model parameter to be null. The safe fix is to always check ModelState.IsValid before using the model. The robust production fix is to use ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-dd) from the client because it is culture-invariant. You can also configure request localisation to match the expected culture, or use a [ModelBinder] attribute with a custom binder that accepts the specific format your UI sends.
keyPoints: The model binder uses the request culture to parse DateTime strings - a culture mismatch causes a parse failure.
A binding failure adds an error to ModelState - always check ModelState.IsValid before using the model.
ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-dd) is culture-invariant and the safest choice for form date inputs.
A non-nullable DateTime property that cannot be set can cause the whole model to come back null.
Alternatives include request localisation configuration or a custom ModelBinder attribute.

code.note: ModelState.IsValid is the safety net that catches every binding failure, including culture-mismatched dates.

answer.analogy: The binder is a translator: give it a date in the wrong language and it hands back an empty page.
redFlags: Not checking ModelState.IsValid and assuming the model parameter is always populated.
Not knowing that DateTime parsing is culture-sensitive in model binding.
Saying the fix is to make the DateTime nullable without explaining that ModelState still needs checking.
Confusing a model binding failure with a validation failure - binding happens before data annotation validation.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between a model binding failure and a validation failure?
followUpsQA.a: Binding failure happens first - the binder cannot convert the string to the target type (for example a bad date format). Validation failure happens after binding succeeds - the bound value violates a data annotation like [Required] or [Range]. Both add errors to ModelState.

followUpsQA.q: How do you configure a specific culture for date parsing?
followUpsQA.a: Add request localisation in Program.cs using app.UseRequestLocalization() with the supported cultures. The model binder will then use the request culture header to select the correct date format.

followUpsQA.q: What is the safest date format to use in an HTML form input?
followUpsQA.a: ISO 8601 (yyyy-MM-dd) using an HTML input type='date' element. All modern browsers send the value in yyyy-MM-dd format regardless of the user's locale, and the .NET model binder parses it without ambiguity.

followUpsQA.q: How do you write a custom ModelBinder for a specific date format?
followUpsQA.a: Implement IModelBinder with a BindModelAsync method that reads the string from the value provider and calls DateTime.TryParseExact with your expected format. Decorate the property or parameter with [ModelBinder(typeof(YourBinder))] to use it.

followUpsQA.q: Can a single property binding failure make the whole model null?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - if the model parameter is a complex type and the binder encounters an error on a non-nullable property, the model parameter can be null depending on the binding configuration. This is why ModelState.IsValid must be checked before any access to the model.

dialogue.text: A date posted from a form makes the entire bound model null. What is going on?

The model binder is trying to parse the posted date string and failing. This usually happens because of a culture mismatch - the form sends '21/06/2026' in dd/MM/yyyy but the server culture expects MM/dd/yyyy. The binder cannot set a non-nullable DateTime, so the whole complex model can end up null. The error ends up in ModelState.
How do you diagnose this in a real application?

First, always check ModelState.IsValid at the top of the action. If it is false, I iterate ModelState.Values and log the errors - the binder adds an entry like 'The value 21/06/2026 is not valid for AppointmentDate'. I can also check the raw request with browser devtools to see exactly what string the form posted.
What is the long-term fix?

Use an HTML input type='date' on the client. Every browser sends that value in ISO 8601 format (yyyy-MM-dd) regardless of the user's locale, and the .NET binder parses it without ambiguity. If I cannot change the client, I configure request localisation on the server to match the expected culture, or I write a custom ModelBinder with DateTime.TryParseExact.
What is the difference between a binding failure and a validation failure?

Binding happens first - converting the raw string to a C# type. If that conversion fails, the property is never set and the error goes to ModelState before any data annotation is ever checked. Validation runs after successful binding against the now-typed value. Both types of failure add errors to ModelState, which is why IsValid catches both.
If you had to support three regional date formats (UK, US, German), how would you handle that?

I would configure request localisation with all three supported cultures using app.UseRequestLocalization(). The browser sends an Accept-Language header, the localisation middleware sets the request culture, and the model binder picks the matching date format. The fallback is to enforce ISO 8601 on the API contract and handle display formatting separately in the UI layer.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
